questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the number of ways in which an examiner can assign 10 marks to 4 questions, giving not less than 2 marks to any questions?
questions.solution:
$x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}+x_{4}=10 \quad x_{i} \geq 2$
$\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}-2\right)+\left(x_{2}-2\right)+\left(x_{3}-2\right)+\left(x_{4}-2\right)=2$
$\begin{array}{llll}\mathrm{y}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{2} & \mathrm{y}_{3} & \mathrm{y}_{4}\end{array}$
$\mathrm{y}_{1}+\mathrm{y}_{2}+\mathrm{y}_{3}+\mathrm{y}_{4}=2 ; \mathrm{y}_{i} \geq 0$
$\therefore$ N.O.W $={ }^{2+4-1} \mathrm{C}_{4-1}={ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{3}=10$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A round table conference is to be held amongst 20 countries. If two particular delegates wish to sit together, then such arrangements can be done in ways.
questions.solution:
We consider two particular delegates as single unit now 19 units can be arranged around a round table in 18! ways and the two delegates can be arranged mutually in 2 ! ways
Hence the required number of ways is $2 \times(18$ !)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A person invites a party of 10 friends at dinner and place them so that 4 are on one round table and 6 on the other round table. The number of ways in which he can arrange the guests is
questions.solution:
Selection of 6 guests $=10 \mathrm{C} 6$
Permutation of 6 on round table $=5 !$
Permutation of 4 on round table $=3 !$
Then, total number of arrangements $=10 \mathrm{C} 6.5 ! .3 !$
$=\frac{(10) !}{6 ! 4 !} \cdot 5 ! .3 !=\frac{(10) !}{24}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The teacher wants to arrange 5 students on the platform such that the boy $\mathrm{B}_1$ occupies second position and the girls $\mathrm{G}_1$ and $\mathrm{G}_2$ are always adjacent to each other, then the number of such arrangements is
questions.solution: There are 5 positions. Given that $\mathrm{B}_1$ occupies $2^{\text {nd }}$ position $\therefore \quad \mathrm{B}_1$ can be arranged in 1 way. As $G_1$ and $G_2$ are always together, none of them can take $1^{\text {st }}$ position. $\therefore \quad \mathrm{G}_1, \mathrm{G}_2$ and one of the remaining students can be arranged on $3^{\text {rd }}, 4^{\text {th }}$ and $5^{\text {th }}$ position when $\mathrm{G}_1$ and $\mathrm{G}_2$ are always together in $2 ! \times 2$ ! Ways. And remaining 2 students can be arranged in 2 ! Ways. $\therefore \quad$ The required number of arrangements $\begin{aligned} & =2 ! \times 2 ! \times 2 ! \\ & =8 \end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of ways in which 5 boys and 4 girls sit around a circular tables. So, that no two girls sit together is
questions.solution:
Number of ways in which the boys can be arranged $=4$ !
Number of ways in which girls alternately arranged $=5$ !
Total number of ways $=5 ! 4 !$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: At an election, a voter may vote for any number of candidates not greater than the number to be elected. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> candidates and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> are to be elected. If a voter votes for at least one candidate, then the number of ways in which he can vote, is
questions.solution:
Total number of ways

=   10 C 1 +   10 C 2 +   10 C 3 +   10 C 4

=10+45+120+210=385
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of zeros at the end of 100! is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{E}_5(100 !)=\left[\frac{100}{5}\right]+\left[\frac{100}{25}\right]+\left[\frac{100}{125}\right]=20+4+0=24$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of four-digit numbers formed by using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn></math> and which are not divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>, is
questions.solution:
No. of four-digit numbers divisible by 5=6+4=10

_ _ _ 0_ ⇒3!=6

_ _ _ 5_ ⇒ 3!-2!=4

Total four-digit numbers =4!-3!=18

∴ Required cases=18-10=8
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If all possible numbers are formed by using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> without repetition and they are arranged in descending order, then the rank of the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>327</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Given numbers are 1, 2, 3, 5, 7.

And also they are arranged in descending order.

i.e., the numbers with four and five digits are included.

The number of five-digit number=5!

The number of four digits number

=p45=5!(5-4)!=1201=120

The number of 3 digits starting with 7=

i.e., 4 digits have to be fit in 2 place in p24 ways,

p24=4!(4-2)!=4×3=12

The number of 3 digits numbers starting with 5=

p24=4!(4-2)!=4×3=12

The number of digits starting with 3

i.e., 3 numbers have to fit in 1 place in p13 way.

p13=3!(3-1)!=3×2×12×1=3=3

The number of digits starting with 3¯5¯ is,

p13=3!(3-1)!=3×2×12×1=3

And the next number is 327.

∴ The rank of 327 is

=120+120+12+12+3+3+1

=271

∴ Rank of 327=271
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If a polygon of $n$ sides has 275 diagonals, then $n$ is equal to
questions.solution:
A polygon of $n$ sides has number of diagonals
$=\frac{n(n-3)}{2}=275 \quad$ (given)
$\begin{array}{cc}\Rightarrow & n^2-3 n-550=0 \\ \Rightarrow & n^2-25 n+22 n-550=0 \\ \Rightarrow & n(n-25)+22(n-25)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (n-25)(n+22)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & n=25,(\because n=-22 \text { is not possible })\end{array}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi>r</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the number of points having position vector <math><mi>p</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi>q</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi>r</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mn>8</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>12</mn></math> are
questions.solution:
8≤p+q+r≤12
⇒p+q+r=8,9,10,11,12
Here, p,q,r≥1
Hence, required number {using number of positive integral solutions = n-1Cr-1 }
=7C2+8C2+9C2+10C2+11C2
=7×62+8×72+9×82+10×92+11×102
=21+28+36+45+55
=185
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12600</mn></math>, the number of even divisors is
questions.solution:
On prime factorization we have, N=12600=23325271.

Number of factors =3+12+12+11+1=72.

Removing power of 2 remaining factors which are odd =2+12+11+1=18.

 ∴  Even factors =72-18=54.

Hence, the answer is 54.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: $S_1, S_2, \ldots \ldots, S_{10}$ are the speakers in a conference. If $S_1$ addresses only after $S_2$, then the number of ways the speakers address is
questions.solution:
Since, $S_1$ speak after $S_2$, therefore two places can be chosen out of 10 place is ${ }^{10} C_2$ ways and rest of the 8 speakers can speak in 8 ! ways.
$\therefore$ Required number of ways $={ }^{10} C_2 \cdot 8$ !
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{10 !}{2 ! 8 !} \cdot 8 ! \\
& =\frac{10 !}{2 !} .
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of ways in which <math><mn>10</mn></math> balls can be selected from <math><mn>10</mn></math> identical green balls, <math><mn>10</mn></math> identical blue balls and <math><mn>9</mn></math> identical red balls are
questions.solution:
Required number of ways = coefficient of x10 in
x0+x1+x2+....+x102x0+x1+x2+....+x9
= coeff. of x10 in 1-x111-x21-x101-x
= coeff. of x10 in 1–x–31–x1121–x10
= coeff. of x10 in 1–x–31+x22–2x111–x10
= coeff. of x10 in 1–x–31+x22–2x11–x10–x32+2x21
= coeff. of x10 in 1–x–31–x10–2x11+2x21+x22–x32
= coeff. of x10 in 1–x–3– coeff. of x0 in 1–x–3
=10+3-1C10-1=12C10-1=12×112–1=65
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If $t_n$ denotes the number of triangles formed with $n$ points in a plane, no three of which are collinear and if $t_{n+1}-t_n=36$, then $n$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$t_n=$ The number of triangles formed with $n$ points in a plane, no three of which are collinear.
i.e.,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& t_n={ }^n C_3 \\
& t_{n+1}={ }^{n+1} C_3
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad t_{n+1}={ }^{n+1} C_3
$$
Now, $t_{n+1}-t_n=36$
$$
\begin{array}{crrl}
\Rightarrow & { }^{n+1} C_3-{ }^n C_3=36 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{(n+1) !}{(n-2) ! 3 !}-\frac{n !}{(n-3) ! 3 !}=36 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{(n+1) n(n-1)}{6}-\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}=36 \\
\Rightarrow & & n(n-1)(n+1-n+2)=36 \times 6 \\
\Rightarrow & & 3 n(n-1)=36 \times 6 \\
\Rightarrow & & n^2-n-72=0 \\
\Rightarrow & & n^2-9 n+8 n-72=0 \\
\Rightarrow & & n(n-9)+8(n-9)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & & (n-9)(n+8)=0 \\
\therefore & & n=9 \quad(\because n \neq-3)
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The least value of the natural number $n$ satisfying $C(n, 5)+C(n, 6)>C(n+1,5)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, }{ }^n C_5+{ }^n C_6>{ }^{n+1} C_5 \\ & { }^{n+1} C_6>{ }^{n+1} C_5 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(n+1) !}{6 !(n+1-6) !}>\frac{(n+1) !}{5 !(n+1-5) !} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 !(n-4) !>6 !(n-5) ! \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 !(n-4)(n-5) !>6 \cdot 5 !(n-5) ! \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n-4>6 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n>10 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n=11 \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If ${ }^n P_r=30240$ and ${ }^n C_r=252$, then the ordered pair $(n, r)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given that, ${ }^n P_r=30240$ and ${ }^n C_r=252$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{n !}{(n-r) !}=30240 \text { and } \frac{n !}{(n-r) ! r !}=252 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad r !=\frac{30240}{252}=120 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad r=5 \\
& \therefore \quad \frac{n !}{(n-5) !}=30240 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)(n-4)=30240 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n(n-1)(n-2)(n-3)(n-4) \\
& \quad=10(10-1)(10-2)(10-3)(10-4) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad n=10
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, required ordered pair is $(10,5)$.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Consider the following statements
i. Number of ways of placing 'n' objects in k bins k≤n ) such that no bin is empty is Ck-1(n-1)

ii. Number of ways of writing a positive integer " n ' into a sum of k positive integers is Ck-1(n-1)

iii. Number of ways of placing ' n ' objects in k bins such that at least one bin is non-empty is Ck-1(n-1)

iv. Ckn-Ckn-1=Ck-1(n-1)
questions.solution:
i. Number of ways of placing 'n' objects in k bins k≤n ) such that no bin is empty is equal to number of integral solution of 

x1+x2+x3+...+xk=n

which is

=Ck-1n-1

ii Let

x1+x2+x3+...+xk=n

Then, number of positive integral solutions are

=Ck-1n-1

iii. Number of ways of placing ' n ' objects in k bins such that at least one bin is non-empty is equal to the number of non-negative integrals solution of

x1+x2+x3+...+xk=n

which is =Ck-1n+k-1

iv

Ckn-Ckn-1=Ck-1(n-1)

∵Ckn=n!(n-r)!r!

LHS

=n!(n-k)!k!-(n-1)!(n-k-1)!k!

=n!n-k·n-k-1!·k!-n-1!n-k-1!·k!

=n!-n-kn-1!n-k·n-k-1!·k!

=n·n-1!-n-kn-1!n-k·n-k-1!·k!

=n-1!n-n-kn-k·n-k-1!·k!

=k·n-1!n-k·n-k-1!·k!

=k·n-1!n-k·n-k-1!·k·k-1!

=(n-1)!(n-k)!(k-1)!=Ck-1n-1
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The total number of ways in which <math> <mrow> <mn>5</mn></mrow> </math> balls of different colours can be distributed among <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn></mrow> </math> persons such that each person gets at least one ball is
questions.solution:
We know that total number of ways of distributing n   distinct things among m persons such that each person gets at least one thing

= m n − C m 1 . ( m−1 ) n + C m 2 ( m−2 ) n − C m 3 ( m−3 ) n +.....

So the required total number of ways

= 3 5 − C 3 1 .2 5 + C 3 2 .1 5 .

=243−96+3

=150
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: How many numbers can be made with the help of the digits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 which are greater than 3000 (repetition is not allowed)
questions.solution:
All the 5 digit numbers and 6 digit numbers are greater than 3000. Therefore, number of 5 digit numbers = 6P5-  5P5=600.

{Since the case that 0 will be at ten thousand place should be subtracted}.

Similarly, number of 6 digit numbers 6! - 5! = 600.

Now, the numbers of 4 digit numbers which are greater than 3000, having 3, 4 or 5 at first place, this can be done in 3 ways and remaining 3 digit may be filled from remaining 5 digits i.e. required number of 4 digit numbers are  5P3×3=180 .

Hence, total numbers =600+600+180=1380
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
